title: PVC Window Box
summary: A window box built from PVC trim board instead of wood, sized to match the width of the window it sits under.
description:
Wood window boxes rot from the inside out — they hold damp soil against end grain for months at a stretch, and even a good finish eventually loses that fight. Building the box from PVC trim board instead removes the problem entirely: PVC doesn't absorb water, so there's no rot risk to manage with paint or sealant in the first place.

Joinery here is **PVC cement, not glue** — a solvent weld that actually fuses the pieces together at the surface, backed up with finish nails for immediate positioning while the weld cures.

**Measure your window's width before cutting anything.** That single measurement sets the length of every long piece in the box; the plan scales directly to whatever window you're building for.

tools.note: Rips the bottom panel to width.

materials.name: Mounting screws (Tapcon for brick, wood screws for siding)

cutList.note: Adjust length to match your window's width.
Mitered lengthwise along the top edge.

steps.title: Measure and cut
steps.body: Measure the window's width — this sets the box's overall length. Cut all PVC pieces per the cut list, mitering the trim pieces as noted.

steps.title: Attach the bottom
steps.body: Glue the ripped bottom panel to one of the long side panels with PVC cement and finish nails, set in 3/4in from the edge.

steps.title: Attach the end panels
steps.body: Attach the two end panels with PVC cement and finish nails to close up the box.

steps.title: Add the trim and mounting cleats
steps.body: Attach the mitered trim pieces around the top edge, and the cleat pieces to the back for mounting.

steps.title: Seal and mount
steps.body: Caulk any remaining seams, then mount to the house with Tapcon screws on brick or wood screws on siding.
materials: Mounting screws (Tapcon for brick, wood screws for siding)
